skill.name: bv
skill.description: Beads Viewer guidance for using the bv CLI to triage Beads issue graphs, rank blockers, inspect cycles, and generate robot-friendly project plans.
skill.summary: Use bv robot commands to analyze Beads dependency graphs and choose high-impact work.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified many local command examples, including bv, bd, jq, shell variables, and command substitution. These are documented CLI workflows rather than hidden executable code, while the weak-cryptography and reconnaissance alerts appear to be false positives from project-analysis terminology and graph export examples. No evidence found of prompt injection, network exfiltration, credential access, or malicious intent in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Directed Local CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run local bv, bd, jq, and shell workflow commands against project files. This is necessary for the advertised workflow, but it can execute installed local binaries and read or modify Beads state, so users should apply it only in trusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain direct command examples and shell command substitution. The risk is real because an agent may run local binaries, but the commands are transparent and aligned with the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Shell Variable and Command Substitution Workflow
medium_findings.description: The workflow captures command output into shell variables and passes derived values to bd claim and bd close. This pattern is common for automation, but it can behave unexpectedly if local command output is malformed or untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution and variable expansion are explicit in the workflow block. There is no evidence of malicious construction, but shell automation still carries input-handling risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography hits occur in descriptive text about graph metrics, robot status fields, recipes, and data_hash output. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, encryption, password hashing, or custom cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these lines are documentation for project graph analysis and output fields. They do not implement or recommend a cryptographic algorithm.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static reconnaissance hits map to graph-analysis terminology such as Topo Sort and Mermaid graph export. No evidence found of host enumeration, network scanning, or system discovery commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content describes dependency graph output formats and graph ordering. It does not instruct system reconnaissance or collect host information.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many Markdown command examples that an agent may execute, including bv robot commands, jq filters, bd state changes, and graph export commands. These should be treated as explicit user-visible external command guidance, not hidden execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is confirmed as local command execution guidance. Context indicates legitimate CLI usage, so it raises publication warnings rather than a block.

content.value_statement: Large Beads projects can hide blockers, cycles, and overloaded dependency paths. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use bv robot commands for deterministic triage and planning.
actual_capabilities: Guides agents to start with bv robot triage commands instead of the interactive TUI.
Explains PageRank, betweenness, HITS, critical path, cycles, density, degree, eigenvector, and topo sort outputs.
Shows how to inspect quick wins, blockers, label health, label flow, and attention-ranked labels.
Documents jq filters for extracting recommendations, status fields, cycles, and label health results.
Provides workflows for claiming, closing, exporting, and comparing Beads graph state.
limitations: Requires the bv CLI and related Beads tooling to be installed in the working environment.
Works only with projects that maintain Beads data such as .beads/beads.jsonl.
Large graphs may return approximate, skipped, or timed-out metrics depending on bv output status.
The skill provides command guidance; it does not include the bv executable or implement graph algorithms itself.

use_cases.title: Prioritize release blockers
use_cases.description: Find high-impact Beads that block other work and choose the next task with graph-backed evidence.

use_cases.title: Coordinate agent work queues
use_cases.description: Use robot outputs to assign ready tasks, avoid dependency cycles, and keep agent work aligned.

use_cases.title: Audit project dependency health
use_cases.description: Review cycles, label flow, bottlenecks, and stale work before planning a sprint or cleanup pass.

prompt_templates.title: Run basic triage
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage for a Beads project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bv skill to run robot triage for this Beads project. Summarize the top recommendation, blockers, and quick wins.

prompt_templates.title: Find the next task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use bv robot mode to identify the single best next Bead to work on. Explain why it is preferred and include any claim command shown by bv.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze graph health
prompt_templates.prompt: Use bv insights to check cycles, bottlenecks, critical path items, and metric status. Report structural problems before recommending work.

prompt_templates.title: Create a parallel execution plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning across labels or tracks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use bv robot planning and label analysis to produce parallel work tracks. Prioritize tasks that unblock other tracks and flag any cycle or timeout issues.

output_examples.input: Triage my Beads project and tell me what to do first.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the top recommended Bead, explains its unblock impact, lists quick wins, and notes whether graph metrics were complete.

output_examples.input: Check whether this sprint has dependency risks.
output: Bottlenecks and critical-path Beads are summarized.
Labels with warning or critical health are called out.

output_examples.input: Plan work for three coding agents.
output_examples.output: The response groups ready Beads into parallel tracks, highlights dependencies between tracks, and recommends which blockers to clear first.

best_practices: Use robot flags for agent workflows because the bare bv command opens an interactive TUI.
Check the status field before trusting advanced graph metrics on large projects.
Resolve cycles before selecting normal implementation work.
anti_patterns: Do not run bare bv in an automated agent session.
Do not ignore skipped or timed-out graph metrics when making priority decisions.
Do not parse beads.jsonl manually when bv robot outputs already provide graph-aware summaries.

faq.question: What does this skill help an agent do?
faq.answer: It helps an agent use bv robot commands to triage Beads projects with graph metrics and deterministic recommendations.

faq.question: Does this skill install bv?
faq.answer: No. It assumes bv and related Beads tools are already available in the environment.

faq.question: Why should agents avoid bare bv?
faq.answer: Bare bv launches an interactive TUI that can block an automated session. Robot flags return machine-readable output.

faq.question: Which graph risks does it emphasize?
faq.answer: It emphasizes cycles, bottlenecks, critical path items, high PageRank blockers, label flow, and dependency density.

faq.question: Can it work on very large projects?
faq.answer: Yes, but some advanced metrics may be approximate, skipped, or timed out. The status field should be checked.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill asks agents to run local CLI commands, so it should be used only where those commands and project files are trusted.
